title: The 20 Most Anticipated Fall Movies and the Winners of the Telluride Film Festival
format: Sean gets back from a 12-hour drive to Telluride with a ranked list of what he saw there; Amanda gets him to relitigate the whole fall calendar, which Paramount has already started rearranging.

segments.heading: Telluride Film Festival Report
segments.note: Sean's four days on the ground, in the order he brings them up

films.title: The Power of the Dog
films.note: Jane Campion's first film since 2009's Bright Star, and possibly the best thing Sean saw all festival. A Netflix release with There Will Be Blood-style anti-western streaks, huge performances from Benedict Cumberbatch and Kirsten Dunst, a Jonny Greenwood score, and Ari Wegner (Zola) on cinematography. Had its big Saturday-night premiere alongside a tribute where Campion received the festival's silver medallion.

films.title: King Richard
films.note: Sean saw this one back in July under embargo. Will Smith as Richard Williams, and Sean's early Best Actor pick: 'very down the middle' as a biopic, but Smith is captivating throughout. The buzziest movie of the festival by his account.

films.title: Cyrano
films.note: Joe Wright's musical adaptation with Peter Dinklage, score by the Dessner brothers of the National. Sean missed it, but says it was well received, and floats Dinklage as a possible Best Actor contender.

films.title: Belfast
films.note: Kenneth Branagh's black-and-white memoir of growing up in Northern Ireland during the Troubles, often compared to Roma. Jamie Dornan, Caitríona Balfe, Judi Dench, and Ciarán Hinds; a lot of Van Morrison on the soundtrack. Sean liked it but was left a little queasy by how sunny a child's-eye view it takes on the surrounding violence. Out November 5th from Focus Features.

films.title: C'mon C'mon
films.note: Mike Mills's new one, black and white, with Joaquin Phoenix as a radio journalist who becomes a surrogate father to his sister's nine-year-old son. Sean admits he can't be objective (Mills was his first-ever interview on the show) but calls it the movie that hit him hardest at the festival, gentle and ambling in the way Beginners and 20th Century Women are.

films.title: The Hand of God
films.note: Paolo Sorrentino's Netflix film, a personal remembrance of his adolescence in Naples as Maradona arrives to play for the city's club.

films.title: Petite Maman
films.note: Céline Sciamma's follow-up, mentioned alongside Belfast and King Richard as part of the festival's father-and-child through-line.

films.title: Cow
films.note: Andrea Arnold's documentary, shot almost entirely on the face of a dairy cow over the course of her life, including two births on camera. A 100-minute, nearly wordless companion piece to last year's Gunda. Sean caught it at 9:30 p.m. and says it hurt.

films.title: Spencer
films.note: Pablo Larraín's Princess Diana film with Kristen Stewart, drawing early Jackie comparisons and real Oscar buzz for Stewart. Sean refuses to say a word about it until Amanda can see it herself.

films.title: The Velvet Underground
films.note: Todd Haynes's documentary on Lou Reed, John Cale, Moe Tucker, and the band's orbit around Andy Warhol's Factory. Sean interviewed Haynes at the festival and calls it the most complete telling of that history he's seen. Hits Apple TV+ October 15th, though he'd rather you see it in a theater.

films.title: The Rescue
films.note: Elizabeth Chai Vasarhelyi and Jimmy Chin's documentary about the rescue of the youth soccer team trapped in a cave in Thailand, from the directors of Free Solo. Archival footage mixed with recreations so well-integrated Sean didn't clock them as recreations until over an hour in. A National Geographic release, and an early Best Documentary contender for him.

films.title: Bergman Island
films.note: Mia Hansen-Løve's film about a couple of filmmakers, played in part by Tim Roth, retreating to Fårö, Ingmar Bergman's home island, to write. Sean flags it as being in dialogue with Olivier Assayas's Non-Fiction, which makes sense since Hansen-Løve and Assayas used to be partners.

films.title: The French Dispatch of the Liberty, Kansas Evening Sun
films.note: Wes Anderson's New Yorker-inspired anthology. Sean loved it after a five-year wait, even though he heard about walkouts and knows plenty of people who found it flat. Says it's probably not anyone's favorite Anderson movie but is still staggering as pure craft.

films.title: The Lost Daughter
films.note: Maggie Gyllenhaal's directorial debut, an Elena Ferrante adaptation. Sean missed it but relays that it announced her as a real filmmaker, with Olivia Colman expected to land another nomination and Jessie Buckley (playing young Colman) picking up some buzz of her own.

films.title: Red Rocket
films.note: Sean Baker's A24 follow-up to The Florida Project, with former MTV VJ Simon Rex as a washed-up porn star drifting back into a red-state Texas town. Sean missed it but notes it produced a lot of walkouts, the same reputation Uncut Gems had at Telluride before it took off.

films.title: A Hero
films.note: Asghar Farhadi's Amazon release. Sean missed it to a schedule conflict and is still annoyed about it.

segments.heading: Ringer Films at TIFF
segments.note: two Ringer Films productions premiering in Toronto, watched from home

films.title: Jagged
films.note: A Ringer Films documentary on Alanis Morissette around the release of Jagged Little Pill, directed by Alison Klayman. Amanda covered the album on 60 Songs That Explain the '90s; Sean says he's heard from a lot of listeners with the same formative-album story she told there.

films.title: Listening to Kenny G
films.note: The other Ringer Films premiere at TIFF, a portrait of Kenny G directed by Penny Lane (Hail Satan?).

films.title: The Humans
films.note: An A24 title also premiering at TIFF; mentioned in passing alongside the rest of that lineup.

segments.heading: The October Bottleneck
segments.note: Paramount bailed on Sean's birthday movie, and everyone else had to recalculate

films.title: Top Gun: Maverick
films.note: Pushed by Paramount from this fall into 2022, along with Jackass Forever and Mission: Impossible – Dead Reckoning Part One. Sean is devastated; he'd been counting on it as a joint birthday movie with Amanda.

films.title: Jackass Forever
films.note: Delayed by Paramount in the same round of pushes as Top Gun: Maverick.

films.title: Mission: Impossible – Dead Reckoning Part One
films.note: The third Paramount delay in the same announcement, referred to on the show simply as 'Mission Impossible 7.'

films.title: Shang-Chi and the Legend of the Ten Rings
films.note: The delays came before this one opened and beat expectations as a theatrical exclusive: proof, Sean argues, that a movie in actual theaters can still work, even mid-Delta-variant. It made roughly half of what a pre-pandemic Marvel movie would have.

films.title: Venom: Let There Be Carnage
films.note: Sony had considered bumping it to 2022 but moved it back to October 1st after Shang-Chi's success.

films.title: No Time to Die
films.note: Still dated for October 8th. Sean is nervous for it, given how many times it's already been delayed and how much a movie this size relies on a clean release.

films.title: Halloween Kills
films.note: Dated for October 15th, the third of the four straight October tentpole weekends.

films.title: Dune
films.note: Closes out the October run on the 22nd, day-and-date on HBO Max. Sean caught it on a Warner Bros. lot pit stop on his way out to Telluride and calls it a mixed bag. A tweet to that effect got him dragged online, though he insists it wasn't meant as a verdict. He's adamant it's Dune Part One, not the whole story, and worried people going in expecting a complete film will be blindsided. Says the practical-and-CGI integration is the best he's ever seen and singles out Oscar Isaac's beard.

segments.heading: Holiday Blockbusters
segments.note: the big swings still on the calendar for November and December

films.title: Eternals
films.note: Chloé Zhao's MCU entry.

films.title: Spider-Man: No Way Home
films.note: Called, without much argument, the most anticipated movie of the year by a lot of people.

films.title: The Matrix Resurrections
films.note: Lana Wachowski directing solo this time, with Keanu Reeves back. A teaser dropped mid-recording and they paused the show to watch it live; Sean is fired up, mostly about the returning Nebuchadnezzar.

films.title: Finch
films.note: Tom Hanks stars in this Apple TV+ release, directed by Miguel Sapochnik (Game of Thrones). Neither host can remember what it was originally called.

segments.heading: Awards Contenders, Confirmed and Rumored
segments.note: everything else on the fall calendar, as of this recording

films.title: Dear Evan Hansen
films.note: Opens September 24th. Ben Platt reprises his Broadway role. Sean saw the trailer and isn't sure what to make of it; both hosts admit their taste in movie musicals skews old-fashioned.

films.title: Titane
films.note: Julia Ducournau's Palme d'Or winner, out October 1st. Sean is looking forward to it despite disliking her earlier film Raw.

films.title: The Last Duel
films.note: Ridley Scott, opening October 15th, with a cast led by Matt Damon. Sean's been talking about it for a while and plans a full episode around it.

films.title: Last Night in Soho
films.note: Edgar Wright's film, warmly received out of Venice with the usual Wright split: people who loved it, people who found it hollow.

films.title: House of Gucci
films.note: Confirmed as happening; Amanda is thrilled, mostly for the cast.

films.title: The Tragedy of Macbeth
films.note: Joel Coen's solo debut, starring Frances McDormand and Denzel Washington, premiering in New York in a few weeks.

films.title: West Side Story
films.note: Steven Spielberg's remake, closing out the year. Neither host is sure a remake was necessary, but they trust Spielberg more than most.

films.title: tick, tick... BOOM!
films.note: Lin-Manuel Miranda's directorial debut for Netflix, starring Andrew Garfield.

films.title: Parallel Mothers
films.note: Pedro Almodóvar's new film, which debuted at Venice. Sean floats an Almodóvar retrospective episode for January.

films.title: Memoria
films.note: Apichatpong Weerasethakul's film with Tilda Swinton, headed to the New York Film Festival.

films.title: Nightmare Alley
films.note: Guillermo del Toro's remake with Bradley Cooper and Cate Blanchett. Dated for December 3rd from Searchlight; no footage has surfaced yet.

films.title: Don't Look Up
films.note: Adam McKay's next film, with Leonardo DiCaprio and Jennifer Lawrence. Both hosts are anticipating it as fans of McKay and the cast.

films.title: Being the Ricardos
films.note: Aaron Sorkin's film about Lucille Ball and Desi Arnaz, one of two Amazon titles Sean has heard, unofficially, will make it out this year.

films.title: The Tender Bar
films.note: George Clooney directs, Ben Affleck stars, adapted from J.R. Moehringer's memoir by Departed screenwriter William Monahan. The other of the two Amazon titles Sean's heard is coming out this year.
